questions.prompt: A bar chart shows applicants by region: Africa 120, Europe 180, Asia 240, Americas 160. Which region has the highest count?
What is the total increase in pass rate from Year 1 to Year 3?
Two trends track completion rates over 6 months. Trend A rises from 50% to 80%. Trend B rises from 40% to 76%. Which has the higher average monthly increase?
questions.rationale: Trend A increases 30/6 = 5 per month; Trend B increases 36/6 = 6 per month.

questions.prompt: Based on the server log below, which endpoint has the worst p99 latency?
questions.rationale: /api/search has a p99 latency of 890ms, the highest.

questions.prompt: This SQL query is meant to find users who signed up in 2025 but never placed an order. Which line has the bug?
questions.context:
SELECT u.id, u.email
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id
WHERE u.created_at >= '2025-01-01'
  AND u.created_at < '2026-01-01'
  AND o.id IS NOT NULL
questions.hint: Think about what LEFT JOIN + IS NOT NULL actually filters.
options: Line 6: o.id IS NOT NULL should be IS NULL
questions.rationale: To find users with NO orders, we need IS NULL (rows where the left join found no match), not IS NOT NULL.

questions.prompt: A pie chart shows time allocation for a developer's day: Coding 40%, Meetings 25%, Code Review 20%, Other 15%. If the workday is 8 hours, how many minutes are spent in meetings?

You have deployment data for 4 teams. Each team deploys once per day. Over 20 workdays, Team A had 2 failures, Team B had 5, Team C had 1, and Team D had 3. If all teams deploy together and a 'bad day' is when ANY team fails, what is the maximum number of completely good days?
questions.rationale: Total failures = 2 + 5 + 1 + 3 = 11. If all failures happen on different days, at most 11 bad days → minimum 9 good days. This is the maximum guaranteed good days.

questions.prompt: If a dataset has a median of 50 and a mean of 65, the distribution is skewed to the right (positively skewed).
questions.rationale: When the mean is greater than the median, the distribution is positively (right) skewed — pulled by high outliers.
